title: Chip-carved spoon back
subtitle: Decorating the back face of a finished wooden spoon with chip-cut patterns
excerpt: Chip carving on the back of a spoon turns a functional utensil into a marked piece. A simple border of triangular chips around the handle, three cuts each, takes twenty minutes on a finished spoon and requires only a sloyd knife and a sharp eye for the cut sequence. This technique covers the chip-cut sequence, grain reading on a curved surface, and the depth rules that prevent splitting.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for chip-carved decoration on small domestic treen. Early-twentieth-century Swedish slöjd manuals for the three-cut chip sequence.

content.text: Chip carving on a spoon back is a three-cut operation repeated across a grid. The result is a border of raised triangles or a field of diamonds, each one defined by two angled slicing cuts and a
stop cut

at the apex. The technique requires a finished, dry, and oiled spoon to work on: the oil stabilises the surface fibres and makes the cuts cleaner. Do not chip-carve green wood; the fibres are too soft and the cuts tear rather than lift.
What it does
A
chip cut

removes a small triangular prism of wood from the surface. Each prism is bounded by the two angled side cuts and the stop cut at its point. Repeated in rows, chip cuts produce a pattern of geometric shadows on the wood surface. The pattern reads clearly because the interior triangle drops below the surface while the surrounding ridges catch light.
When to use it

After a spoon is carved, dried, and oiled but before the oil is fully cured. The oil makes the surface slightly slick, which is exactly what is needed for clean chip cuts. Chip carving also works on flat surfaces: box lids, picture-frame faces, and the handles of mallets or drawknife handles.
What you need

A finished, dry, lightly oiled spoon in a close-grained wood: sycamore, birch, cherry, or beech. A sloyd knife or chip-carving knife that is extremely sharp; the cuts must be clean without tearing. A strop to maintain the edge; strop after every five chips. Pencil and a small ruler for marking the grid.
Step-by-step
Step 1: Mark the grid

Mark a grid of equilateral triangles along the handle back using a pencil and small ruler. A triangle with 8 mm sides is a good starting size for a first attempt; smaller triangles require a finer knife tip and more controlled cuts. Mark lightly; the pencil lines are removed by the final sand.
Step 2: The stop cut at the apex

Place the knife point at the apex of the first triangle (the deepest point of the chip) and press straight down into the wood, perpendicular to the surface. The stop cut is 2 to 3 mm deep. This cut defines the boundary that the next two slicing cuts will meet. Without it, the slicing cuts tear past their intended end and the chip comes out ragged.
Step 3: First slicing cut

Hold the knife at forty-five degrees to the surface. Place the blade on the left side of the triangle, at the base corner, and draw it in a slicing cut toward the stop cut at the apex, angling the blade slightly under the chip. The blade should reach the stop cut at 2 to 3 mm depth at the apex and emerge at the surface at the base corner. The grain direction on the handle back runs along the handle; a slicing cut angled across the grain on this face will lift the chip more cleanly than a straight push cut.
Step 4: Second slicing cut

Mirror the first cut on the right side of the triangle, working from the right base corner to the stop cut at the apex. The chip should lift cleanly as this second cut meets the first and the stop cut. If the chip does not lift, a third very shallow pass along the base of the triangle (connecting the two base corners) releases it.
Step 5: Repeat across the grid

Work row by row across the grid. Alternate the apex direction in each row so the chips interlock: one row pointing toward the bowl, the next pointing toward the handle tip. This interlocked pattern produces the classic diamond-point chip-carving border.
What it looks like when it is right

Each chip has clean, straight walls meeting at a sharp apex. The base of each chip is slightly concave (a natural result of the angled slicing cuts meeting below the surface). Viewed at a low angle to the light, the pattern casts clean triangular shadows with no torn fibres at the edges.
Common mistakes
Skipping the stop cut: the slicing cuts tear past the apex and split the ridge between chips. Always make the stop cut first, before either slicing cut.

Going against the grain on the slicing cut: the chip tears and leaves a rough pit instead of a clean prism. The slicing cut must travel in the direction that peels the grain, not against it. If the chip tears, reverse the approach direction.
Chips deeper than 4 mm on a spoon handle: the handle wall is thin at the neck and deep chips will break through. Keep chips to 2 to 3 mm on thin sections.
A dull knife: the single most common cause of torn chips. Strop every five cuts; for fine chip carving, the knife must be sharper than for any other spoon-carving operation.
